title: The neuroscience of safety signals and why they matter
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your brain is always scanning for danger 

 Here's the thing. Your nervous system is doing something right now, in this exact moment, that you probably ar...
htmlContent:
Your brain is always scanning for danger

Here's the thing. Your nervous system is doing something right now, in this exact moment, that you probably aren't aware of. It's scanning. Constantly. For safety. For danger. For anything that might tell it whether you can relax or whether you need to prepare for the worst.

This scanning happens below your conscious awareness. You don't decide to do it. It's just happening. Your brain is picking up on cues in your environment, in the people around you, in the tone of my writing, in the temperature of the room. And it's using those cues to make a split-second decision: safe or not safe.

I want to talk about what happens when those cues go wrong. When your nervous system keeps getting signals that say "danger" even when you're actually fine. And more , I want to talk about what you can do about it.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, called these cues "neuroception." It's the way your nervous system detects safety and threat without involving your conscious brain. Think about that for a second. Your body is making decisions about whether you're safe before you even have a chance to think about it. Wild, right?

And here's where it gets complicated. If you grew up in an environment that wasn't safe, your neuroception got calibrated differently. Your nervous system learned to see danger everywhere. Because at one point, that was actually true. Danger was everywhere. And your brain did exactly what it was supposed to do, it kept you alive.

But now? Now you're sitting in a coffee shop or on your couch or in your car, and your body is still acting like a predator is around the corner. That's not your fault. That's your nervous system doing its job based on old information.

So what do we do about this? We start with safety signals.

What are safety signals and why does your nervous system need them?

A safety signal is any cue that tells your nervous system "you can rest now." It could be the sound of someone's voice. The weight of a blanket. The smell of a familiar space. The feeling of your feet on the floor. The sight of a trusted face. The rhythm of your own breath.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talked about how trauma gets stuck in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical sensation, a frozen response, a nervous system that hasn't finished what it started. And one of the ways to help it finish is by offering it safety signals.

I remember working with a client who couldn't sleep without every light on in the apartment. She knew it wasn't rational. She knew no one was coming. But her body didn't know that. Her body was still in the apartment she grew up in, where darkness meant danger. We worked on finding one safety signal she could trust. Just one. For her, it was the sound of a fan. A consistent, predictable hum that told her body "you're not alone, there's something here with you."

That's the thing about safety signals. They don't have to be big. They don't have to be . They just have to be consistent. Your nervous system learns through repetition. If you keep giving it the same signal, over and over, in the same context, it starts to believe it. It starts to downshift from defense mode into rest mode.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down exactly how to build these signals into your daily life. It's not theoretical. It's practical. It's the kind of book you can actually use.

But here's what no one tells you. Safety signals can feel uncomfortable at first. Really uncomfortable. Because if your nervous system has been in high alert for years, decades even, the idea of dropping that vigilance can feel like letting your guard down. It can feel dangerous in itself. Stay with me here. That discomfort is normal. It doesn't mean you're doing it wrong. It means your system is learning a new language.

Why your nervous system resists safety (and why that's not your fault)

I want to be honest with you. Sometimes safety signals don't work. Or they work for a while and then stop. Or they work in one context but not another. And when that happens, it's easy to think "I'm broken" or "this doesn't work for me."

But that's not what's happening. What's happening is that your nervous system has a very strong memory of what danger looks like. And it's not going to give up that memory easily. Because that memory, as painful as it is, kept you alive. Your brain is not going to throw away a survival strategy just because you bought a weighted blanket and downloaded a meditation app.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively about trauma and the body, talks about how trauma changes the brain. It literally rewires your neural pathways. The parts of your brain that detect threat get bigger, stronger, more sensitive. The parts that help you feel safe and connected get smaller, weaker, less responsive. This is not a character flaw. This is neurobiology.

So when you try to introduce a safety signal and your body says "nope, I don't trust this," that's not failure. That's your brain doing exactly what it learned to do. The question is not "how do I make this go away." The question is "how do I work with this resistance."

One approach that helps many people is understanding the bigger picture of how trauma shapes your life. A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift alone can change how you relate to your own resistance.

Here's another way to think about it. Imagine you've been walking through a dark forest for years. You've learned to listen for every crack of a twig, every rustle of leaves. You've learned to stay alert because that's how you survived. Then someone comes along and says "hey, you can relax now, we're in a meadow." Your body doesn't know what a meadow is. Your body knows the forest. And it's going to keep listening for those twig cracks because that's what it knows. That's what it trusts.

Building safety signals is not about forcing your body to trust the meadow. It's about slowly, gently, repeatedly showing your body that the meadow is real. That the twig cracks are just twig cracks. That you are not in the forest anymore.

How to build safety signals that actually work for you

So how do you do this? How do you build safety signals when your nervous system doesn't trust anything?

You start small. Really small. Not "I'm going to meditate for 20 minutes" small. I mean "I'm going to notice the feeling of my breath for three seconds" small. Think about it. You're not trying to change your whole nervous system in one go. You're just trying to give it one tiny piece of information that says "maybe this is okay."

Here are some safety signals that people actually use. Not the ones that wellness influencers tell you to use. The ones that real people with real trauma have found helpful.

The weight of your own hand on your chest. The feeling of a wall at your back. The sound of your own voice humming a single note. The sight of a specific color that you associate with calm. The texture of a familiar fabric. The taste of something sour or cold. The pressure of your feet against the floor.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did any of those examples make you feel something? Even a tiny shift? That's your nervous system responding. That's a signal landing.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. It explores how trauma can be passed down through generations and how to identify the patterns that might be running in your family system. Sometimes understanding where the resistance comes from makes it easier to work with.

The key is consistency over intensity. A small safety signal that you use every day is worth more than a big dramatic intervention that you try once and then abandon. Your nervous system learns through repetition. Through predictability. Through "oh, this thing again, and nothing bad happened last time, so maybe I can relax a little."

And please, please don't force it. If a safety signal feels bad, stop using it. Your body knows what it needs. You don't have to use someone else's safety signal. You don't have to use the ones I listed. You get to discover your own. You get to experiment. You get to be curious about what works for you.

What to do when safety signals aren't enough

I want to be honest about something. Sometimes safety signals are not enough. Sometimes your nervous system is so dysregulated, so stuck in survival mode, that no amount of humming or hand-on-chest is going to bring it back. And that's okay. That's not a personal failure. That's a sign that you need more support.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a light read. It's dense and sometimes heavy. But it validates what many of us have experienced. It names the connection between what happened to us and what's happening in our bodies. And that validation alone can be a safety signal.

If you find that your nervous system is consistently unable to settle, even with safety signals, please consider working with a therapist who understands trauma. Not just any therapist. Someone trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or another body-based approach. Someone who understands that trauma lives in the nervous system, not just in the stories we tell about it.

You deserve that support. You deserve to not have to figure this out alone. Safety signals are a tool, not a cure. They are part of a larger process of healing that often requires professional guidance.

I'll leave you with this. Your nervous system is not your enemy. Even when it feels like it's working against you, even when it keeps you stuck in hypervigilance or collapse, it is trying to protect you. It is doing the best it can with what it knows. And with time, with patience, with small consistent signals, it can learn new patterns. It can learn that you are safe now. It can learn to rest.

But that learning takes time. It takes repetition. It takes gentleness. And it takes you showing up, again and again, offering your nervous system the same quiet message: you are okay. You are okay. You are okay.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system by Deb Dana
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Exploring inherited trauma patterns by Mark Wolynn
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - A foundational book on trauma and the body by Bessel van der Kolk
